title: How We Built Runtime Security for AI Agents
content:
AI agents are going to production. And most of them have no security layer underneath them.
Over the past eight months, my co-founder Orobosa and I have been building Vaultak; runtime security and behavioral monitoring for AI agents. This post is about what we built, why we built it, and the technical decisions we made along the way.

**

## The Problem We Kept Seeing
**
The pattern is always the same. A team ships an AI agent into production;  it writes to a database, sends emails, calls external APIs. Things work fine in testing. Then in production, the agent does something unexpected. A record gets deleted. PII gets sent somewhere it should not. A loop runs hundreds of times because a condition was never met.
The frustrating part is not that these things happen. It is that there was no layer in place to catch them before damage was done.
Traditional application security is built around humans doing things. Firewalls, WAFs, access controls; they are all designed for predictable, structured requests. AI agents are different. They are non-deterministic. The same input can produce a different action depending on context, model state, and tool availability. Standard security tooling has no model for this.
That is the gap Vaultak closes.

**What Vaultak Actually Does**
At its core, Vaultak is a behavioral interception and policy enforcement layer. It sits between your agent and the systems it controls, and it does four things:

Monitors every action the agent takes in real time
Scores behavioral risk on a 0–10 scale across multiple dimensions
Enforces policy;  blocking or requiring approval for out-of-bounds actions
Rolls back automatically when a risk threshold is breached

The SDK integration is two lines of code:
pythonfrom vaultak import Vaultak

vt = Vaultak(api_key="vtk_...")

with vt.monitor("my-agent"):
    # your agent code here
    pass
Everything inside the monitor context is wrapped. Vaultak intercepts actions, scores them, applies your policy, and either allows, blocks, or rolls back;  all before your production systems are touched.

**The Risk Scoring Engine**
This was the hardest part to build well.
The naive approach is to build a rule-based system;  a blocklist of dangerous actions. But rule-based systems are brittle. An agent can cause significant damage with actions that look completely benign in isolation. Deleting a single row is low risk. Deleting rows in a loop across a production table is not.
We built a five-dimension scoring model:
1. Action Type
What category of action is this? Read operations score low. Write operations score higher. Delete, drop, and truncate operations score highest. We maintain a taxonomy of action types and their base risk scores.
2. Resource Sensitivity
What is being acted on? An agent writing to a logging table is different from an agent writing to a user credentials table. We classify resources by sensitivity tier;  public, internal, sensitive, and critical.
3. Blast Radius
How much damage could this action cause if it goes wrong? An action affecting one row has a small blast radius. An action affecting all rows in a table has a large one. We estimate blast radius from the scope of the operation.
4. Frequency
How often is this action being taken? A single write is expected. A hundred writes in ten seconds is a signal. We track action frequency over a rolling time window and score anomalies.
5. Context Deviation
Does this action fit the behavioral pattern we have observed for this agent? If an agent has never sent an external email before and suddenly tries to, that is a deviation worth flagging; even if email sending is not explicitly blocked.
Each dimension produces a sub-score. The overall risk score is a weighted average. When the score crosses your configured threshold, Vaultak intervenes.

**Policy Enforcement**
The policy layer is where teams express their intent about what an agent is and is not allowed to do.

pythonvt = Vaultak(
    api_key="vtk_...",
    policy={
        "block": ["delete", "drop_table", "send_external_email"],
        "require_approval": ["write_production_db"],
        "max_risk_score": 7.0
    }
)
Three policy modes:

Block;  the action is stopped immediately, the agent receives a policy violation response
Require approval;  the action is paused and a human approval request is sent before execution continues
Alert;  the action is allowed but logged and surfaced as a high-priority event in the dashboard

The approval workflow was something we added after talking to security teams. They did not want to just block agents;  they wanted to stay in the loop for high-stakes operations without creating so much friction that the agent became unusable. The pause-and-approve pattern solved that.

**Automatic Rollback**
Blocking is reactive. Rollback is recovery.
When an agent breaches a risk threshold mid-operation, Vaultak does not just stop it; it attempts to undo what has already been done. Every action within a monitored context is logged with full state context: what was the state before the action, what changed, and what would need to happen to reverse it.
This is harder for some action types than others. Database writes are straightforward to roll back. Emails that have already been sent are not. We handle this by classifying actions as reversible or irreversible at the point of interception, and applying a stricter threshold to irreversible actions before they execute.

**PII Masking**
One of the most common failure modes we observed was agents leaking sensitive data through their outputs; sending PII to external APIs, logging it in plain text, or including it in LLM prompts where it should not be.
We built a PII masking layer that runs on agent inputs and outputs:
pythonfrom vaultak import mask_pii

safe_output = mask_pii(agent_output)
SSNs, emails, credit cards, phone numbers, names masked automatically
Under the hood this uses a combination of regex patterns for structured PII (SSNs, credit card numbers, phone numbers) and a lightweight NER model for unstructured PII (names, addresses). The masking is applied before data leaves the monitored context.

**MCP Gateway Scanning**
Model Context Protocol is becoming the standard for how agents connect to tools. With MCP, an agent can call hundreds of external tools through a single interface; which is powerful, and also a significant attack surface.
We built an MCP gateway scanner that intercepts all tool calls before they execute and checks them against a policy ruleset. Prompt injection attempts embedded in tool responses, calls to unauthorized tools, and anomalous tool usage patterns are all caught at the gateway level.

**Vaultak Sentry; Zero Code Integration**
Not every team wants to touch their agent code. Security engineers especially; they want visibility across everything running in an environment without waiting for every development team to integrate an SDK.
Sentry is a desktop daemon that monitors agent activity at the system level. Install it, connect it to your Vaultak dashboard, and get full behavioral visibility across every agent running on the machine; with zero code changes required.
It works by intercepting system-level network and process activity and correlating it with known agent behavioral signatures. The result is the same risk scoring and alerting you get from the SDK, without touching a single line of agent code.
Download it at vaultak.com/download; available for macOS, Windows, and Linux.

**The GitHub Action**
One thing we shipped recently that has been well received: a GitHub Action that scans agent code for security risks on every pull request.

yaml- name: Vaultak Security Scan
  uses: samueloladji-beep/vaultak-action@v1
  with:
    api_key: ${{ secrets.VAULTAK_API_KEY }}

It runs static analysis on your agent code, flags high-risk patterns, and blocks merges that introduce critical security issues. Catching problems at the PR stage is significantly cheaper than catching them in production.

**What We Learned**
A few things that surprised us during the build:
The entry point is the security engineer, not the CTO. We initially assumed we would sell top-down. In practice, the developers and security engineers who are closest to the problem are the ones who find Vaultak, integrate it, and then advocate for it internally. Bottom-up distribution works in this space.
Observability is not enough. A lot of tools will tell you what an agent did. Fewer will stop it from doing something it should not. The teams we talk to want active intervention, not just logging.
The blast radius framing resonates. When we describe the problem as "what is the worst thing this agent could do if it misbehaves"; the blast radius question; engineers immediately understand why a security layer is necessary. That framing unlocked a lot of conversations.

**Try It**
Vaultak is live and available now.

SDK: pip install vaultak
Sentry: vaultak.com/download
Docs: docs.vaultak.com
GitHub: github.com/samueloladji-beep/Vaultak

The Starter plan is free. No credit card required.
If you are deploying AI agents in production and you do not have a security layer underneath them, Vaultak is worth ten minutes of your time to evaluate. The blast radius of getting this wrong is significant.

Samuel Oladji is the CEO and co-founder of Vaultak. Orobosa Ekhator is the CTO and co-founder.
